summary: O'Neil voices support for the growing standardized testing opt-out movement, arguing that schools spend too much time on testing. She notes that the movement is gaining traction not just in New York but also in Florida, where limits on testing hours were recently enacted. While acknowledging that Common Core and over-testing are separate issues, she understands why parents conflate them. She encourages participation in a Brooklyn rally for the opt-out movement.
key_points: The standardized testing opt-out movement is growing nationally, with both New York and Florida taking action
Too much classroom time is devoted to testing, making opt-out a reasonable response
Common Core concerns and over-testing are distinct issues but understandably conflated by frustrated parents
Even conservative states like Florida are pushing back against excessive standardized testing
money_quotes: There's just too much time spent in our schools on testing, so pulling our kids out of that complete waste is an obvious step in the right direction.
And although I see lots of reasons to discuss Common Core issues separately from the over-reliance on testing, I can understand why parents are wrapping all of that stuff up together.
In Florida, of all places, they recently limited the number of hours kids can be made to take standardized tests.
key_insight: The widespread parental revolt against standardized testing reflects legitimate concerns about how much instructional time is being sacrificed to assessment.
